name: Algebra II
description: Advanced algebra extending into functions, polynomials, rational expressions, exponential and logarithmic functions, sequences, series, probability, and conic sections.

modules.title: Review: Linear & Quadratic Functions
modules.objectives: Graph linear and quadratic functions; convert to vertex form; calculate the discriminant; solve real-world quadratic problems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 5.1: Quadratic Functions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Vertex Form of a Quadratic
modules.assignment: Graph 5 quadratic functions in vertex form and 5 in standard form. For each, identify the vertex, axis of symmetry, direction of opening, and discriminant. Write a real-world problem modeled by a quadratic and solve it.

modules.title: Polynomial Functions
modules.objectives: Describe end behavior using leading coefficients; find zeros and their multiplicities; apply the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra; sketch polynomial graphs.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 5.3: Graphs of Polynomial Functions
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Polynomial Zeros and Graphs
modules.assignment: For 6 polynomial functions (degree 3–5), determine end behavior, find all real zeros with multiplicities, and sketch the graph. Verify using Desmos. State how many complex zeros each polynomial must have.

modules.title: Dividing Polynomials
modules.objectives: Divide polynomials using long division and synthetic division; apply the Remainder Theorem and Factor Theorem to find zeros.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 5.4: Dividing Polynomials
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Dividing Polynomials: Long and Synthetic Division
modules.assignment: Divide 5 polynomials using long division and 5 using synthetic division. Use the Remainder Theorem to evaluate p(c) for 3 polynomials. Use the Factor Theorem to determine whether given binomials are factors of given polynomials.

modules.title: Rational Expressions & Equations
modules.objectives: Simplify rational expressions; multiply and divide rational expressions; solve rational equations; identify vertical and horizontal asymptotes.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 5.6: Rational Functions
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Rational Expressions and Equations
modules.assignment: Simplify 6 rational expressions. Multiply and divide 4 pairs of rational expressions. Solve 6 rational equations, checking for extraneous solutions. For 3 rational functions, identify all asymptotes.

modules.title: Radical Functions & Rational Exponents
modules.objectives: Simplify radical expressions; convert between radical and rational exponent form; solve radical equations; identify extraneous solutions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 1.3: Radicals and Rational Exponents
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Radical Functions and Rational Exponents
modules.assignment: Simplify 8 radical expressions. Rewrite 6 expressions converting between radical and rational exponent form. Solve 6 radical equations, checking for extraneous solutions. Graph y = √x and y = ∛x and compare domains.

modules.title: Exponential Functions
modules.objectives: Identify exponential growth and decay; graph exponential functions; apply to compound interest and population models.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 6.1: Exponential Functions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Exponential Growth and Decay
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Exponential Growth and Decay Models
modules.assignment: Graph 4 exponential functions (2 growth, 2 decay), identifying key features. Solve 3 compound interest problems using A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt). Solve 2 population growth problems. Determine whether each function represents growth or decay.

modules.title: Logarithmic Functions
modules.objectives: Define logarithms as inverses of exponential functions; apply properties of logarithms; solve logarithmic equations; work with natural log.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 6.3–6.5: Logarithmic Functions and Properties
modules.assignment: Convert 8 expressions between exponential and logarithmic form. Expand and condense 6 logarithmic expressions using properties. Solve 6 logarithmic equations. Evaluate 4 natural log expressions.

modules.title: Exponential & Logarithmic Applications
modules.objectives: Use exponential and logarithmic models for half-life, pH, earthquake magnitude, and continuous growth; solve applied problems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 6.7: Exponential and Logarithmic Models
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Exponential and Logarithmic Applications
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Exponential and Log Models Practice
modules.assignment: Solve 2 half-life problems. Calculate pH for 3 substances using pH = −log[H⁺]. Compute earthquake magnitudes using the Richter scale formula. Solve 2 continuous growth problems using A = Pe^(rt).

modules.title: Sequences & Series
modules.objectives: Identify arithmetic and geometric sequences; find general terms; use sigma notation; evaluate finite and infinite geometric series.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 9.1–9.5: Sequences and Series
modules.assignment: Identify whether 5 sequences are arithmetic, geometric, or neither. Write the nth term formula for each. Use sigma notation to express and evaluate 3 series. Determine whether 3 infinite geometric series converge and find their sums.

modules.title: Conic Sections
modules.objectives: Identify and graph parabolas, circles, ellipses, and hyperbolas from standard form equations; write equations given key features.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 10.1–10.4: Conic Sections
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Conic Sections
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Conic Sections: All Four Curves
modules.assignment: Graph one parabola, one circle, one ellipse, and one hyperbola from given equations. For each, identify key features (vertices, foci, asymptotes where applicable). Write the standard form equation of each conic given its key features.

modules.title: Probability
modules.objectives: Apply the Fundamental Counting Principle; calculate permutations and combinations; compute basic probability using the addition and multiplication rules.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 11.5–11.7: Counting and Probability
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Permutations, Combinations, and Probability
modules.assignment: Apply the Counting Principle to 4 problems. Compute 5 permutations and 5 combinations using formulas. Calculate probabilities for 6 events using addition and multiplication rules. Solve 2 problems involving complementary events.

modules.title: Statistics: Data & Distributions
modules.objectives: Calculate mean, median, and standard deviation; describe normal distributions; use z-scores to compare data.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 11 & Statistics Supplement: Data and Distributions
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Normal Distribution and Z-Scores
modules.assignment: Compute mean, median, and standard deviation for 3 data sets. Draw and label a normal distribution curve. Calculate z-scores for 5 data values and interpret each. Determine what percentage of data falls within 1, 2, and 3 standard deviations.

modules.title: Matrices
modules.objectives: Add, subtract, and multiply matrices; compute determinants of 2×2 and 3×3 matrices; solve systems of equations using matrix equations and Cramer's Rule.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 9 Supplement: Matrices and Determinants
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Matrix Operations and Determinants
modules.assignment: Perform addition, subtraction, and multiplication on 4 pairs of matrices. Calculate the determinant for 3 matrices of each size (2×2 and 3×3). Solve 3 systems of equations using the inverse matrix method.

modules.title: Systems of Equations & Inequalities
modules.objectives: Solve linear systems in 3 variables; solve systems of linear inequalities; apply linear programming to optimization problems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Algebra and Trigonometry 2e — Ch 9 Supplement: Systems and Linear Programming
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Systems of Equations and Linear Programming
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Systems of Equations Practice
modules.assignment: Solve 4 three-variable systems by elimination. Graph and find feasible regions for 3 systems of linear inequalities. Set up and solve 2 linear programming problems, identifying the objective function, constraints, and optimal solution.

questions.question: Which of the following is the vertex form of a quadratic function?
questions.explanation: Vertex form is y = a(x − h)² + k, where (h, k) is the vertex of the parabola.

questions.question: What is the vertex of f(x) = 2(x − 3)² + 5?
questions.explanation: In vertex form y = a(x − h)² + k, the vertex is (h, k). Here h = 3 and k = 5, so vertex is (3, 5).

questions.question: Describe the end behavior of f(x) = −2x³ + x − 4.
options: As x → ∞, f → ∞ and as x → −∞, f → −∞
As x → ∞, f → −∞ and as x → −∞, f → ∞
questions.answer: As x → ∞, f → −∞ and as x → −∞, f → ∞
questions.explanation: Leading term is −2x³. Odd degree with negative leading coefficient: falls right, rises left.

questions.question: Divide: (x³ − 2x² − 5x + 6) ÷ (x − 3) using synthetic division.
questions.explanation: Synthetic division with 3: coefficients 1, −2, −5, 6 → 1, 1, −2, 0. Quotient: x² + x − 2.

questions.question: Simplify the rational expression: (x² − 4) / (x² − x − 2).
questions.explanation: Factor numerator: (x+2)(x−2). Factor denominator: (x−2)(x+1). Cancel (x−2). Result: (x+2)/(x+1).

questions.question: Simplify: ∛(x⁶y³).
A population triples every 4 years. Starting at 500, which function models the population after t years?
questions.explanation: Every 4 years the population is multiplied by 3. The growth factor 3 is raised to the power t/4.

questions.question: Evaluate: log₂(64).
questions.explanation: log₂(64) = x means 2^x = 64 = 2⁶, so x = 6.

questions.question: Which property of logarithms is used to expand log(ab)?
questions.explanation: The Product Rule states log(ab) = log(a) + log(b).

questions.question: Solve for x: log₃(x) = 4.
questions.explanation: log₃(x) = 4 means 3⁴ = x. 3⁴ = 81.

questions.question: Solve: (x + 2)/(x − 1) = 3.
questions.explanation: Cross multiply: x + 2 = 3(x − 1) = 3x − 3. So −2x = −5; x = 5/2.

questions.question: Which equation has a vertical asymptote at x = 2?
questions.explanation: Vertical asymptotes occur where the denominator equals zero. x − 2 = 0 gives x = 2.

questions.question: Solve: √(2x + 3) = 5.
questions.explanation: Square both sides: 2x + 3 = 25. 2x = 22; x = 11. Check: √(22 + 3) = √25 = 5. ✓

questions.question: Explain the relationship between exponential functions and logarithmic functions. Give one example of each and show they are inverses of each other.
questions.answer: Logarithmic functions are the inverses of exponential functions. For example, f(x) = 2^x and g(x) = log₂(x) are inverses because f(g(x)) = 2^(log₂x) = x and g(f(x)) = log₂(2^x) = x.
questions.explanation: The inverse relationship means their graphs are reflections across y = x, and composing them returns the original input.

questions.question: $1,200 is invested at an annual interest rate of 5%, compounded quarterly. Write the formula and compute the value after 3 years (round to the nearest cent).
questions.explanation: Using A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt): P = 1200, r = 0.05, n = 4, t = 3. (1.0125)^12 ≈ 1.16075, so A ≈ $1,392.90 (accept reasonable rounding).

questions.question: What is the discriminant of 3x² − 4x + 2 = 0, and how many real solutions does it have?
questions.explanation: b² − 4ac = (−4)² − 4(3)(2) = 16 − 24 = −8. Negative discriminant means no real solutions.

questions.question: A polynomial p(x) has zeros at x = −2 (multiplicity 2) and x = 3 (multiplicity 1). Which describes its graph?
options: Touches the x-axis at −2, crosses at 3
Crosses the x-axis at −2, touches at 3
Touches the x-axis at both −2 and 3
questions.answer: Touches the x-axis at −2, crosses at 3
questions.explanation: Even multiplicity → graph touches (bounces). Odd multiplicity → graph crosses. x = −2 has multiplicity 2 (even), x = 3 has multiplicity 1 (odd).

questions.question: Use the Factor Theorem: Is (x + 2) a factor of p(x) = x³ − x² − 4x + 4?
Which is a correct simplification of (x² − 9)/(x² + 6x + 9)?
questions.explanation: Numerator: (x+3)(x−3). Denominator: (x+3)². Cancel one (x+3): (x−3)/(x+3).

questions.question: Solve: √(x − 1) = x − 3.
questions.explanation: Square both sides: x − 1 = (x−3)² = x² − 6x + 9 → x² − 7x + 10 = 0 → (x−2)(x−5) = 0. Check x = 2: √1 = −1 (false). x = 5: √4 = 2 ✓. Only x = 5.

questions.question: Convert 16^(3/4) to simplest radical form.
A radioactive substance decays according to A(t) = 200e^(−0.035t). What is the half-life (to the nearest year)?
Condense: 2log(x) + log(y) − log(z).
questions.explanation: Power rule: 2log(x) = log(x²). Product rule: log(x²) + log(y) = log(x²y). Quotient rule: log(x²y) − log(z) = log(x²y/z).

questions.question: Find the 8th term of the arithmetic sequence: 3, 7, 11, 15, …
Find the sum of the infinite geometric series: 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 + …
The standard form equation of an ellipse with center at the origin, horizontal major axis of length 10, and minor axis of length 6 is:
questions.explanation: Semi-major axis a = 5 (horizontal), semi-minor axis b = 3. Standard form: x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 = x²/25 + y²/9 = 1.

questions.question: A class of 15 students must elect a president, vice-president, and treasurer (no person can hold two offices). How many ways can this be done?
questions.explanation: Order matters → permutation. P(15, 3) = 15!/(15−3)! = 15 × 14 × 13 = 2730.

questions.question: A data set has mean 70 and standard deviation 5. What z-score corresponds to a value of 82?

Multiply the matrices: [2  1] × [3  0]. (First row of each; treat as 1×2 × 2×1 for the (1,1) entry.)
                     [1  4]   [−1 2]
questions.explanation: The (1,1) entry of the product is 2·3 + 1·(−1) = 6 − 1 = 5.

questions.question: Which system of inequalities has the feasible region in the first quadrant bounded by y ≤ 4 and x + y ≤ 6?
questions.explanation: First quadrant requires x ≥ 0 and y ≥ 0. Adding y ≤ 4 and x + y ≤ 6 creates the bounded feasible region.

questions.question: Solve the system: x + y + z = 6, 2x − y + z = 3, x + 2y − z = 2.
questions.explanation: Adding equations 1 and 3: 2x + 3y = 8. From eq 1 and 2: 3x + 2z = 9 (after eliminating y). Solving gives x = 1, y = 2, z = 3. Verify in all three equations.

questions.question: A substance has a pH of 3.5. Given pH = −log[H⁺], find the hydrogen ion concentration [H⁺] in scientific notation. What does this pH value indicate about the substance?
questions.explanation: pH = −log[H⁺] → log[H⁺] = −3.5 → [H⁺] = 10^(−3.5) ≈ 3.16 × 10⁻⁴. pH < 7 indicates an acid.

questions.question: A ball is launched upward from a cliff 80 ft high with initial velocity 64 ft/s. Its height is h(t) = −16t² + 64t + 80. Find the maximum height and the time at which it is reached.
questions.answer: Maximum height is 144 ft at t = 2 seconds.

questions.question: A student earns scores of 72, 85, 90, 68, and 95 on five tests. Calculate the mean and standard deviation. Would a score of 60 be considered unusual? Explain using z-scores.
questions.explanation: A z-score beyond ±2 is generally considered unusual in a normal distribution.

quizQuestions.question: What is the axis of symmetry of f(x) = x² − 6x + 8?
quizQuestions.explanation: Axis of symmetry: x = −b/(2a) = −(−6)/(2·1) = 3.

quizQuestions.question: A quadratic opens downward when:
quizQuestions.explanation: When the leading coefficient a is negative, the parabola opens downward.

quizQuestions.question: The discriminant of x² − 4x + 4 = 0 is:
quizQuestions.explanation: b² − 4ac = 16 − 4(1)(4) = 16 − 16 = 0. One repeated real root.

quizQuestions.question: Convert f(x) = x² − 4x + 7 to vertex form.
quizQuestions.explanation: Complete the square: (x² − 4x + 4) + 3 = (x − 2)² + 3.

quizQuestions.question: A ball is thrown upward from 5 ft high with height h(t) = −16t² + 32t + 5. What is the initial height?
quizQuestions.explanation: Initial height is h(0) = −16(0) + 32(0) + 5 = 5 ft.

quizQuestions.question: The end behavior of f(x) = 4x⁵ − x is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Degree 5 (odd) with positive leading coefficient: falls left (x→−∞) and rises right (x→∞).

quizQuestions.question: How many zeros (counting multiplicity) does a degree-4 polynomial have?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra states a degree-n polynomial has exactly n zeros (counting multiplicity, including complex zeros).

quizQuestions.question: A zero with even multiplicity means the graph:
options: Touches and bounces at the x-axis
quizQuestions.answer: Touches and bounces at the x-axis
quizQuestions.explanation: Even multiplicity → the graph touches the x-axis but does not cross it.

quizQuestions.question: The polynomial f(x) = (x − 1)²(x + 3) has zeros at:
quizQuestions.explanation: Set each factor to zero: x − 1 = 0 → x = 1; x + 3 = 0 → x = −3.

quizQuestions.question: Which polynomial has end behavior: as x → ∞, f → ∞ and as x → −∞, f → ∞?
quizQuestions.explanation: Even degree with positive leading coefficient → both ends rise.

quizQuestions.question: According to the Remainder Theorem, if p(x) is divided by (x − c), the remainder equals:
quizQuestions.explanation: The Remainder Theorem: dividing p(x) by (x − c) gives remainder p(c).

quizQuestions.question: Perform synthetic division of x³ + 3x² − x − 3 by (x − 1). What is the quotient?
quizQuestions.explanation: Coefficients: 1, 3, −1, −3. Divide by 1: bring 1 down. 1+3=4, 4·1=4; −1+4=3, 3·1=3; −3+3=0. Quotient: x² + 4x + 3.

quizQuestions.question: The Factor Theorem states that (x − c) is a factor of p(x) if and only if:
quizQuestions.explanation: By the Factor Theorem, (x − c) is a factor exactly when p(c) = 0.

quizQuestions.question: Divide: (2x² + 5x − 3) ÷ (x + 3) using long division. The remainder is:
quizQuestions.explanation: 2x² + 5x − 3 = (x + 3)(2x − 1). So (x + 3) divides evenly; remainder = 0.

quizQuestions.question: If p(2) = 5 and p(x) is divided by (x − 2), what is the remainder?
quizQuestions.explanation: By the Remainder Theorem, the remainder when dividing by (x − 2) is p(2) = 5.

quizQuestions.question: To simplify a rational expression, you factor the numerator and denominator and then:
options: Set the expression equal to zero
quizQuestions.explanation: Cancelling common factors in numerator and denominator simplifies the rational expression.

quizQuestions.question: Simplify: (2x² − 8)/(x − 2).
What is the vertical asymptote of f(x) = 3/(x + 5)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Vertical asymptotes occur where the denominator is zero: x + 5 = 0 → x = −5.

quizQuestions.question: Solve: 1/x + 1/3 = 5/6.
quizQuestions.explanation: Multiply through by 6x: 6 + 2x = 5x → 6 = 3x → x = 2.

quizQuestions.question: The horizontal asymptote of f(x) = (3x² + 1)/(x² − 4) is:
quizQuestions.explanation: When degrees are equal, horizontal asymptote = ratio of leading coefficients = 3/1 = 3.

quizQuestions.question: Simplify: √(50x²).
quizQuestions.explanation: √50 = 5√2; √(x²) = |x| = x (assuming x ≥ 0). Result: 5x√2.

quizQuestions.question: Rewrite x^(2/3) in radical form.
Solve: √(x + 5) = 3.
quizQuestions.explanation: Square both sides: x + 5 = 9 → x = 4. Check: √(4+5) = √9 = 3 ✓.

quizQuestions.question: What is the domain of f(x) = √(x − 4)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The expression under a square root must be ≥ 0: x − 4 ≥ 0 → x ≥ 4.

quizQuestions.question: Which is an extraneous solution of √(2x − 1) = x − 2?
quizQuestions.explanation: Squaring gives 2x − 1 = x² − 4x + 4 → x² − 6x + 5 = 0 → x = 1 or x = 5. Check x = 1: √1 = 1 but x − 2 = −1. Extraneous.

quizQuestions.question: The function f(x) = 3^x represents:
quizQuestions.explanation: A base greater than 1 gives exponential growth.

quizQuestions.question: What is the y-intercept of f(x) = 5 · 2^x?
quizQuestions.explanation: f(0) = 5 · 2⁰ = 5 · 1 = 5. y-intercept is (0, 5).

quizQuestions.question: $500 is invested at 6% compounded annually. What is the amount after 2 years?
Which function represents exponential decay?
quizQuestions.explanation: A base between 0 and 1 gives exponential decay.

quizQuestions.question: The horizontal asymptote of f(x) = 2^x + 3 is:
quizQuestions.explanation: As x → −∞, 2^x → 0, so f(x) → 3. Horizontal asymptote: y = 3.

quizQuestions.question: Which property states log(xⁿ) = n·log(x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Power Rule of logarithms: log(xⁿ) = n · log(x).

quizQuestions.question: Expand: log₂(8x).
quizQuestions.explanation: Product rule: log₂(8x) = log₂(8) + log₂(x) = 3 + log₂(x).

quizQuestions.question: Solve: ln(x) = 2.
quizQuestions.explanation: ln(x) = 2 means e² = x. x = e² ≈ 7.389.

quizQuestions.question: Solve: log(x) + log(x + 3) = 1.
quizQuestions.explanation: log[x(x+3)] = 1 → x(x+3) = 10 → x² + 3x − 10 = 0 → (x+5)(x−2) = 0. x = −5 is extraneous (log of negative). x = 2.

quizQuestions.question: Carbon-14 has a half-life of 5,730 years. After 11,460 years, what fraction of the original amount remains?
quizQuestions.explanation: 11,460 years = 2 half-lives. After 2 half-lives: (1/2)² = 1/4 of original remains.

quizQuestions.question: A substance with [H⁺] = 0.001 mol/L has pH =:
An earthquake measures 7.0 on the Richter scale. Another measures 5.0. The first is how many times more intense?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Richter scale is logarithmic: difference of 2 means 10² = 100 times more intense.

quizQuestions.question: A population grows continuously at 4% per year. Starting with 1,000, the model is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Continuous growth uses A = Pe^(rt) where r = 0.04.

quizQuestions.question: Solve for t: 100 = 50·e^(0.02t).
Find the common difference of the sequence: 5, 9, 13, 17, …
Find the common ratio of the geometric sequence: 2, 6, 18, 54, …
Evaluate: Σ(k=1 to 4) of 3k.
Does the infinite geometric series 1 + 1/3 + 1/9 + … converge? If so, what is its sum?
Which formula gives the nth term of a geometric sequence with first term a₁ and common ratio r?
quizQuestions.explanation: The nth term of a geometric sequence: aₙ = a₁ · r^(n−1).

quizQuestions.question: Which conic section is represented by x² + y² = 25?
quizQuestions.explanation: x² + y² = r² is the standard form of a circle centered at the origin with radius r = 5.

quizQuestions.question: What are the foci of the ellipse x²/25 + y²/16 = 1?
quizQuestions.explanation: c² = a² − b² = 25 − 16 = 9 → c = 3. Foci: (±3, 0) along horizontal major axis.

quizQuestions.question: The parabola y² = 8x opens:
quizQuestions.explanation: Parabola of form y² = 4px opens right when p > 0. Here 4p = 8 so p = 2 > 0.

quizQuestions.question: What are the asymptotes of the hyperbola x²/9 − y²/16 = 1?
quizQuestions.explanation: Asymptotes of x²/a² − y²/b² = 1 are y = ±(b/a)x. Here b = 4, a = 3. y = ±(4/3)x.

quizQuestions.question: Write the equation of a circle with center (−2, 3) and radius 5.
A restaurant offers 3 appetizers, 5 entrees, and 2 desserts. How many different meals (one of each) are possible?
quizQuestions.explanation: Fundamental Counting Principle: 3 × 5 × 2 = 30.

quizQuestions.question: How many ways can 4 students be arranged in a line from a group of 10?
How many ways can a committee of 3 be chosen from 8 people?
A bag has 4 red and 6 blue marbles. What is the probability of drawing a red marble?
Two dice are rolled. What is the probability that the sum is 7?
Which measure of center is most affected by outliers?
quizQuestions.explanation: The mean uses all data values and is sensitive to extreme values (outliers).

quizQuestions.question: In a normal distribution, approximately what percent of data falls within one standard deviation of the mean?
quizQuestions.explanation: The empirical (68-95-99.7) rule: approximately 68% falls within ±1σ of the mean.

quizQuestions.question: A data set has mean 50 and standard deviation 10. What z-score corresponds to a value of 35?
Which describes a data set with a large standard deviation?
options: Data clustered close to the mean
Data spread widely around the mean
quizQuestions.answer: Data spread widely around the mean
quizQuestions.explanation: Standard deviation measures spread. Large σ means values are spread far from the mean.

quizQuestions.question: In a normal distribution with mean 100 and standard deviation 15, approximately what percent of values fall between 70 and 130?
What is the size of the product of a 2×3 matrix and a 3×4 matrix?
quizQuestions.explanation: Matrix product of m×n and n×p gives an m×p matrix. 2×3 · 3×4 = 2×4.

quizQuestions.question: What is the determinant of the matrix [[3, 1], [2, 4]]?
Two matrices A and B can be added if and only if they:
options: Have the same size (same rows and columns)
quizQuestions.answer: Have the same size (same rows and columns)
quizQuestions.explanation: Matrix addition is defined only for matrices with identical dimensions.

quizQuestions.question: The identity matrix I satisfies:
quizQuestions.explanation: Multiplying any matrix A by the identity matrix I gives A. AI = IA = A.

quizQuestions.question: To solve the system AX = B using matrices, we compute:
quizQuestions.explanation: Multiply both sides of AX = B on the left by A⁻¹: X = A⁻¹B.

quizQuestions.question: The solution to a system of 3 equations in 3 variables is:
quizQuestions.explanation: A system of 3 equations in 3 unknowns has a solution that is an ordered triple.

quizQuestions.question: In linear programming, the optimal value always occurs at:
options: The center of the feasible region
A corner (vertex) of the feasible region
The midpoint of a boundary edge
quizQuestions.answer: A corner (vertex) of the feasible region
quizQuestions.explanation: The Extreme Value Theorem for linear programming: optimal solutions occur at vertices of the feasible region.

quizQuestions.question: Which ordered triple satisfies the system: x + y + z = 6, x − y = 1, z = 2?
Which region is the solution to the system: y ≤ 2x + 1 and y ≥ −x + 3?
options: To the right of both lines
quizQuestions.explanation: The solution is where y is both ≤ 2x+1 (below the first line) and ≥ −x+3 (above the second line) — the region between them.

quizQuestions.question: A factory makes two products. Product A earns $5 profit and Product B earns $8 profit. If x is the number of A and y is the number of B, which is the correct objective function to maximize?
quizQuestions.explanation: Objective function for profit: P = (profit per A)·x + (profit per B)·y = 5x + 8y.
